I will not join the chorus of enthusiastic reviews. A very long movie with elaborate double crossings, eventually reveals that the winners are two young women who instead of harming one another, as it was the plan drawn by evil men, develop a lesbian romance and come on top , in many ways than one. Photography is excellent. With the many flashbacks it becomes increasingly difficult to understand in what phase the two protagonists are currently engaged. There is a generous amount of female nudity, including a long scene of lesbian sex in several positions. Other movies have been accused of gratuitous content, but Mr Wook apparently has solid credentials.

Although a few characters and scenes could have been handled a tad better, The Handmaiden is yet another breathtaking achievement from Park Chan-wook that has excellent cinematography, stunningly complex storytelling and terrific performances, while having a lot of rich thematic depth and a surprisingly great score.

The Handmaiden, adapted from Sarah Waters' Fingersmith, masterfully transposes the 19th-century London setting to 1930s Japanese-occupied Korea, creating a rich and visually stunning historical backdrop. Told in three intricately woven chapters, the film unfolds with gripping suspense, emotional depth, and narrative twists that keep viewers fully engaged. With award-winning cinematography, meticulous artistic direction, and powerful performances—especially from debut actress Kim Tae Ri—The Handmaiden stands out as a bold, sensual, and unforgettable cinematic achievement that expertly blends mystery, romance, and rebellion. 🏳️‍🌈🇰🇷 An erotic historical psychological thriller directed, co-written and co-produced by Park Chan-Wook, inspired by the 2002 novel Fingersmith by Welsh writer Sarah Waters. The story transposes the Victorian-era setting to Korea under Japanese colonial rule, where a young Korean woman is hired as a handmaiden to a reclusive Japanese heiress living on a countryside estate. But the maid harbors a secret: she is a pickpocket enlisted by a conman to help seduce the Lady and steal her fortune. It is a masterfully crafted piece of cinema — perhaps longer than necessary, but executed with exceptional precision. The narrative is divided into three intricately structured parts, each unfolding with elegance and surprise, anchored by stunning performances. The musical score is hauntingly beautiful, perfectly timed with each twist and emotional shift. Visually, it is nothing short of breathtaking: rich with evocative landscapes, meticulous lighting and colour, and sumptuous production design. With its layers of dark humor, sensuality, betrayal, and revenge, it is a daring and unforgettable film — romantic, provocative, and quite refined.

So good. There’s a lot to give away so I’ll keep it brief. This movie utilizes twists and reframings incredibly effectively, using them to explore every emotional avenue of every character in really unique ways. Park Chan-Wook does an excellent job of conveying information without being heavy handed, opting to show rather than tell, which is becoming quite rare in mystery films like this one. The plot is just complex enough to be interesting without feeling convoluted. The connections of the characters will satisfy. Plus, this is a great one for Pride Month. Enjoy.

I found myself ensnared in a mesmerizing dance of deception and desire. With every twist and turn, I was drawn deeper into the labyrinthine web of secrets and motives that propelled the characters forward, eagerly unraveling the layers of subterfuge alongside them. Yet, amidst the gripping suspense and masterful storytelling, it was the exploration of love in its myriad forms that resonated most deeply with me. The blossoming relationship between Sook-hee and Lady Hideko unfolded with exquisite tenderness and raw emotion, each moment laden with the weight of societal expectations and the yearning for connection.

Transported from Victorian-era Britain to Japanese-ruled colonial Korea of the 1930s, Park Chan-wook’s adaptation of Sarah Waters' 2002 novel Fingersmith is a triumph of erotica, so luxurious the production, so delicious the performances and so gripping the narration that the tale of treacherous double crosses of love and money revolving the titular maiden, her mistress and their devious captors never dulls but endlessly intoxicates.

There are some daring and explicit scenes that were really surprising. This movie just could not leave my head the day after I watched it. Directing and shooting the same scenes twice but each from a different perspective... so great! Perfectly structured, it is an incredible work of writing, direction and performance.

With "The Handmaiden," Park Chan-wook delivers a solid — albeit occasionally indulgent — crime thriller, nailing the twists and turns with skill and framing it all with a deft hand. Call me puritan all you want, man, I just found this to be a bit *too* horny at times. Obviously some of the ideas at hand are purposefully perverse and controversial, but with the way some scenes and shots lingered, I couldn't help but feel a bit uncomfortable by things. Make no mistake, this isn't pornographic or anything. I just think that certain ideas behind certain scenes could have been streamlined. Aside from that, however, I had a good time with this. The narrative is exquisitely dark, yet tender and well-constructed. You gotta love double-crosses that are followed swiftly by a double-double cross. Again, solid stuff overall. Just not sure I'd ever be able to recommend this to my parents without blushing.
